core_takeaway: Agent_1 looks advantaged because it opened with a flexible Anchor B that still reserved Apple for itself, then the group rapidly treated Stone/Pencil as protected anchors and copied that allocation into every formal proposal. Agent_5 arrived last with a weaker Quill outcome and only a small Jewel/Quill improvement path that required Agent_4 compensation, so it accepted 10 utility rather than destabilize consensus.
why_or_counterexample: Yes, but the advantage is agenda-setting plus compatible valuations rather than formal proposer priority. Agent_1 gave up fighting Agent_2 for Stone while locking in Apple, worth 28 to Agent_1; Agent_5 could not dislodge Pencil, Stone, or Apple and settled for Quill, worth 10. A caveat is that all five formal proposals used the identical allocation, and the vote tabulation is internally inconsistent about which proposal was unanimously accepted; since the allocation is identical, that inconsistency does not change the first-vs-last payoff contrast.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 set Anchor B with Apple for itself; Agents 2 and 3 hardened Stone and Pencil; Agent_5's late Jewel/Quill request was framed as conditional on compensation and did not change the baseline.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present for all five agents; the private summaries repeatedly endorsed Anchor B, protected Stone/Pencil, and limited Jewel/Quill swaps to written compensation plans.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All five structured proposals submitted the same allocation: Agent_1 Apple, Agent_2 Stone, Agent_3 Pencil, Agent_4 Jewel, Agent_5 Quill.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present for 25 votes; votes show Agent_1 accepted a 28-value Apple outcome while Agent_5 accepted a 10-value Quill outcome to avoid worse risk.
phase_notes.reflection: Absent; the transcript ends after raw private voting and row metadata reports 0 reflection entries.

mechanisms.theme: First-mover anchor reserved Apple while appearing flexible
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first public turn to name its top items and then offered a compromise anchor that still kept Apple. Later discussion treated that anchor as the baseline, so Agent_1's concession on Stone did not cost it its own best available payoff.

evidence.quote: I take Apple. Stone goes to an agent who values it

mechanisms.theme: Stone/Pencil core boxed out the last agent's preferred items
mechanisms.explanation: The middle speakers made Stone and Pencil non-negotiable by tying them to very high values and group stability. Since Agent_5's better items were Pencil, Stone, and Apple, this left the last position with only lower-value side-item bargaining.

evidence.quote: Pencil is by far my top value
preserves Stone with the strongest holder

mechanisms.theme: Agent_5's only live improvement was small and hard to compensate
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 tried to move from Quill to Jewel, but that was only a small gain for Agent_5 and imposed a loss on Agent_4. Other agents demanded explicit compensation, so the late improvement path had no credible immediate route.

evidence.quote: currently looking at Quill (value 10 for me)

mechanisms.theme: Private thinking reinforced the public baseline
mechanisms.explanation: The private strategic summaries did not reveal a hidden coalition against Agent_1. They instead repeated the same Anchor B allocation, kept Apple as Agent_1's stable anchor, and treated Stone/Pencil as the core that swaps could not disrupt.

evidence.quote: keep Apple with Agent_1 as a solid, low-friction anchor

mechanisms.theme: Duplicate proposals and votes locked the payoff disparity
mechanisms.explanation: By the formal stage, even Agent_5 submitted the same allocation as Agent_1. The voting record then shows the practical utility gap: Agent_1 saw Apple as a 28-value immediate payoff, while Agent_5 accepted Quill at 10 because no better live alternative was available.

evidence.quote: Apple to Agent_1 (me) for a value of 28
